ontology.foodex2_description: Fish cake — deep-fried fish paste product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP is for 'Fish cake', which is a general category. This specific Thai fish cake (Tod Mun Pla) with red curry paste, kaffir lime, and long beans is a more specific preparation not separately coded. Therefore, A0FKP is considered a 'closest_parent' match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai fish cake as a distinct food concept.

main_ingredients: fish paste (white fish such as dory or snapper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces of Thai fish cake weighing approximately 160 g, as commonly sold at food delivery outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish cake, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for fried fish products
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Thai-style fish cake (Tod Mun Pla). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried fish cake / fish patty entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore fried fish product data. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. Energy calculated from macros: protein 17.5 g × 4 + fat 18.75 g × 9 + carbohydrate 11.25 g × 4 = 70 + 168.75 + 45 = 283.75 kcal/100g; input states 281.25 kcal — within acceptable rounding tolerance. Values accepted as medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using fried fish cake / fish patty category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai fish cake (Tod Mun Pla). GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous fried fish cake / fish patty entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The low GI reflects the high protein content (17.5 g/100g) and moderate carbohydrate content (11.25 g/100g) from starch binders and long beans, which slow gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 40 × (11.25 × 1.6) / 100 = 40 × 18.0 / 100 = 7.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 593.75 mg/100g — near the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold, driven by fish sauce and red curry paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit portion size to no more than 1–2 pieces per occasion and request reduced fish sauce in preparation, as a full 4-piece serving (160 g) delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (11.25 g/100g) and high protein (17.5 g/100g) with high fat from deep-frying (18.75 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai fish cake has a low estimated GI (~40) and high protein content that slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose rise; however, the high fat content from deep-frying (18.75 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption, so individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to occasional servings and monitor cumulative sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.75 g/100g), saturated fat (5.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.13 g/100g), and sodium (593.75 mg/100g) from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g, trans fat at 0.13 g/100g, and sodium at 593.75 mg/100g makes this food a high cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should avoid regular consumption and opt for baked or pan-fried fish cake preparations with reduced fish sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat (0.13 g/100g) amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE generation and trans fat content; patients with RA should avoid this preparation and choose baked or steamed fish cake made from omega-3-rich fish such as salmon or mackerel.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai fish cake (Tod Mun Pla / ทอดมันปลา) is a traditional Thai street food and appetiser consisting of seasoned fish paste patties deep-fried until golden brown and crispy on the outside while remaining tender and springy inside. The paste is typically made from white fish (such as dory or snapper), blended with red curry paste, finely sliced kaffir lime leaves, long beans, egg, and fish sauce, then shaped into small round patties and deep-fried in vegetable oil. In Singapore, this dish is commonly sold at Thai food stalls and via food delivery platforms as a 4-piece serving (~160 g). The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic from the kaffir lime and curry paste, with a firm, bouncy texture characteristic of fish paste products. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 281 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 18.75 g fat (5.0 g saturated, 0.13 g trans), and 11.25 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 593.75 mg/100g due to fish sauce and curry paste. The dish is clinically relevant for hypertension (near-threshold sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (deep-frying AGEs). The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), making postprandial glucose impact moderate when consumed in standard portions. Common allergens include fish, egg, and gluten from fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai fish cake (Tod Mun Pla) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from fried fish cake / fish patty composites in USDA SR Legacy and cross-referenced with HPB fried fish product data. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Thai-style fried fish cake not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in available approved sources for this food type. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for Thai-style fried fish cake not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 17.5, fat_g 18.75, carbohydrate_g 11.25) are non-zero and consistent with a deep-fried fish paste product; no zero-macro justification required. Input record name '105D Homemade Fish Cake (4pcs) 自制鱼饼 (四件)' retained as alias; canonical food name normalised to 'Thai Fish Cake'. Ethnic tag 'Pad Thai' removed from cultural context as it is not relevant to this dish. Country of origin listed as Singapore in input (sold in Singapore) but dish origin is Thailand; region_of_origin set to Thailand accordingly. Trans fat value in input is 0.13 g/100g (not 0.3 g/100g as stated in input health_suitability narrative — the 0.3 figure appears to be a rounding error in the narrative; 0.13 g/100g used as the authoritative per_100g value).
